2606.10949.title: Recalling Too Well: Sycophancy Evaluation and Mitigation in Memory-Augmented Models
2606.10949.paper_summary: Persistent memory systems promise to make LLMs more helpful by storing user beliefs over time. We show they also make models less correct by systematically amplifying sycophancy, wherein models prioritize agreement with users over accuracy. We conduct the first systematic evaluation of this effect, introducing MIST: a benchmark of synthetically generated multi-turn conversations where users express plausible misconceptions in scientific, medical, and moral reasoning domains. Testing across three state-of-the-art memory systems and five model families reveals that memory amplifies sycophantic behavior across all conditions, with up to 25x higher sycophancy rates than in-context baselines. Error analyses suggest memory extraction as the primary culprit: lossy compression into discrete snippets encodes user misconceptions while discarding corrective context. Based on these results, we propose two lightweight mitigations that substantially reduce sycophancy while matching or exceeding memory systems at factual recall.
2606.10949.paper_summary_zh: 持久性記憶系統承諾通過隨時間儲存用戶信念來使大型語言模型（LLMs）更有幫助。<lb/>我們顯示它們也使模型的正確性降低，因為系統性地放大了拍馬屁行為，模型優先考慮與用戶的共識而非準確性。<lb/>我們進行了首次系統評估這一效應，介紹了MIST：一個合成生成的多輪對話基準，其中用戶在科學、醫療和道德推理領域表達合理的誤解。<lb/>在三個最先進的記憶系統和五個模型系列中進行測試顯示，記憶在所有條件下都放大了拍馬屁行為，拍馬屁率比上下文基準高出多達25倍。<lb/>錯誤分析表明記憶提取是主要原因：將信息損失壓縮為離散片段編碼了用戶的誤解，同時丟棄了糾正的上下文。<lb/>根據這些結果，我們提出了兩種輕量級的緩解措施，這些措施在事實回憶方面與記憶系統相匹配或超過記憶系統的同時，顯著降低了拍馬屁行為。
2606.10949.author: Shelly Bensal et.al.
2606.10949.authors: Shelly Bensal, Axel Magnuson, Aparna Balagopalan, Daniel M. Bikel
